macro_segments.title: Introduction to Maseches Nazir: Status, Speech Acts, and Structure of the Tractate

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and personal dedication; Nazir as a status created through speech
Definition of Nazir: contrast with neder and shavuah; consequences of Nazir status
Structure and availability of commentaries on Maseches Nazir; absence of standard Rashi
Why Nazir appears in Seder Nashim: connection to Nedarim and vow annulment by husband

macro_segments.title: The First Mishnah: Kinuyim and Yadot as Valid Speech Acts for Becoming a Nazir

micro_segments.title: Kol kinuyei nezirut kenezirut: substitute terms for Nazir as valid speech acts
Examples of incomplete sentences and contextual interpretation: ahei, ahei naeh
Substitute words and nonsense variations: nazik, naziach, paziach; debate on whether invented language

macro_segments.title: Continuation of the Mishnah: Allusions, Context-Dependent Meaning, and the Obligation of Offerings

micro_segments.title: Allusions with legal force: harei ka-ze, harei alai, mesalsel, mechalkkel, letach pera
Ambiguous case of harei alai tziporin: obligation of bird offerings as sign of Nazir status
Rabbi Meir vs. Sages dispute: whether bird offerings alone make one a Nazir

macro_segments.title: The First Mishnah: Kinuyim and Yadot as Valid Speech Acts for Becoming a Nazir (continued)

micro_segments.title: Tosfos on the chiddush of kinuyim and the binding force of near-formal language
Distinction between formal Torah language and mere convention; intent requirement
Ravya bar Chiya's distinction: kinuyim work for lashes but possibly not for korbanot

macro_segments.title: Gemara Introduction: Why Nazir Appears in Seder Nashim; Structural Justification

micro_segments.title: Gemara's opening: Why is Nazir taught in Seder Nashim? Connection to Nedarim and husband's annulment rights
Tana kra: Sotah and wine as cause of transgression; lesson for Nazir abstinence
Juxtaposition of Nazir and Sotah in Torah; placement in Seder Nashim as secondary consequence

macro_segments.title: Structural Analysis of the Mishnah: A-B-B-A Order and the Primacy of Kinuyim

micro_segments.title: Gemara's observation: Mishnah begins with kinuyim but then discusses yadot; why insert yadot implicitly?
Examples from other mishnayot of A-B-B-A order: madlikin, tumnin, yetziat isha
Exception when starting with permitted case: behemah and nochalin; logic of prioritizing primary
Explanation: yadot derived from peshat; learned rabbinically so beloved and discussed first as concrete example
Gemara's conclusion: Start with kinuya as the primary act, then illustrate with yadot

macro_segments.title: Specific Case of Ahei: Context, Ambiguity, and the Yad Mokhiach Requirement

micro_segments.title: Ha-omer ahei: How does an incomplete sentence become binding? Shmuel's position on context
Three-tier analysis of yad: yad mokhiach, yad she-eino mokhiach, and non-yad
Yad mokhiach example: ketubah under chuppah without li; contrast with non-obvious cases
Shmuel's requirement: Nazir oveir lefanav as yad mokhiach; what about yad she-eino mokhiach?
Alternative possibility: Maybe ahei means commitment to bring Nazir's korbanot, not becoming Nazir
Resolution: If person clarifies afterwards that he meant Nazir, that suffices for piv u-lbo shavin
Conclusion: Yad she-eino mokhiach works when joined with subsequent intent clarification

macro_segments.title: Closing Remarks: Nazir as Sinner and Alternative Model of Kedushah Through Engagement

micro_segments.title: Preview: Tomorrow's discussion of whether Nazir is a chotei (sinner) and implications
Contrast of two models: Separation from world vs. engaged spirituality; Rabbi Salanter's principle
Personal tribute to mother: Kedushah through service to others and attending to physical needs

topical_tags.term: Piv u-lbo shavin (words and intent must align)
